speaker: SPEAKER_01

speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: But in your talk, you also talked about the idea of having a declarative style of building logic, which is what Jetpack Compose is aiming towards, versus the traditional form, which is imperative.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: I want to ask what your thoughts were about the style of building things.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Declarative versus imperative.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: The last time I heard a big conversation around this was around the whole functional versus imperative aspect, right?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So where does all that fit in?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Yeah, I mean, one thing that I like to try and point out is that...
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Imperative is not a bad word, right?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Like, I think that there's a tendency for people to get really excited about these types of frameworks.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And, you know, we call them declarative, which is, you know, for the most part, correct.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: But I think that people start associating, oh, declarative good, imperative bad.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And I don't think that's necessarily true or how you should be thinking about it.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: You know, declarative, one way to think about declarative versus imperative is that declarative is like strictly less things to describe.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: There's, if something is declarative, you're leaving the
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: you're leaving this sort of how left up to the framework to decide.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Whereas in an imperative style, it's much more like the framework saying, I'm not smart, I'm letting you tell me exactly how to do it.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And so when you have a declarative system, you're kind of by definition, you're giving up some control, you're relinquishing some control of how something happens
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: to the underlying system.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And sometimes that dramatically simplifies how you write the code, which is good.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: But other times it makes implementing very nuanced things more difficult.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And so this is like one of those things, like animation is something where it can be very difficult to describe certain animations in a declarative system because
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: One of the things that a declarative UI framework is typically doing is saying, I'm going to take care of the state transitions for you.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So you just tell me what state A is and state B, and I'll figure out how to update everything to be in state B now.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And which is great, but a lot of times you want to animate from state A to state B.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And so then the system has to allow you to specify exactly how it's doing that thing, where the whole point earlier was to not tell it how to do that thing.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And so that's like one of the challenges is there are certain pockets of UI development that actually end up being harder.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: in a declarative system.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And so we have to think really hard about the right APIs to expose to allow people to do those things when they need to, but not removing or going back on all the gains that we had made in making the common case so much simpler.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: That makes sense.

text: That makes sense.
